info.name: Symfony API
info.description:
# Symfony API — Complete Reference

## Overview

The **Symfony API** is a RESTful web service built on the [Symfony PHP framework](https://symfony.com/), one of the most mature, battle-tested, and enterprise-grade PHP frameworks available. Symfony is the foundation of many major PHP projects (including Laravel itself, Drupal, and Magento) and is renowned for its strict architecture, reusable components, dependency injection container, and long-term support (LTS) releases.

This API exposes structured application data over HTTP in JSON format and follows REST (Representational State Transfer) architectural principles:

- Each **URL** identifies a unique resource or collection of resources (e.g., `/api/categories` represents the full collection of categories; `/api/categories/1` identifies the single category with ID 1).
- **HTTP methods** define the action performed on the resource: `GET` retrieves data without side effects, `POST` creates a new resource, `PATCH` partially updates an existing resource, `DELETE` permanently removes a resource.
- **Responses are stateless**: every request is fully self-contained. The server does not maintain session state between requests — each call must include all information the server needs to process it.
- **Responses are self-describing**: the HTTP status code, response headers, and JSON body together fully communicate the outcome of every request.

This collection documents every available endpoint in the Symfony API, including required headers, full request body schemas with data types and validation rules, complete response structures with field-by-field explanations, all relevant HTTP status codes, and practical usage guidelines.

The API is currently configured for **local development** and runs on your machine at `http://127.0.0.1:8000` (or equivalently `http://localhost:8000`). All API endpoints are prefixed with `/api/`.

---

## Base URL

```
http://127.0.0.1:8000
```

All endpoint paths in this collection are relative to this base URL. To avoid hardcoding this value in every request, create a Postman environment variable named `base_url` with the value `http://127.0.0.1:8000` and reference it as `{{base_url}}/api/...` in all request URLs.

---

## Endpoints Summary

| # | Name | Method | Path | Description |
|---|------|--------|------|-------------|
| 1 | Get Categories | `GET` | `/api/categories` | Retrieve all categories as a JSON array |
| 2 | Post Category | `POST` | `/api/categories` | Create a new category resource |
| 3 | Single Category | `GET` | `/api/categories/{id}` | Retrieve one category by its numeric ID |
| 4 | Update Category | `PATCH` | `/api/categories/{id}` | Partially update an existing category by ID |
| 5 | Delete Category | `DELETE` | `/api/categories/{id}` | Permanently delete a category by ID |

---

## Required Request Headers

Every request to this API must include the correct HTTP headers. Missing or incorrect headers are the most common source of unexpected errors.

### `Accept: application/json`

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Header name** | `Accept` |
| **Header value** | `application/json` |
| **Required for** | All requests — GET, POST, PATCH, DELETE |

The `Accept` header tells the server what media type the client can understand and process. When set to `application/json`, Symfony's exception listener and response serializer return all responses — including validation errors, not-found errors, and server errors — as structured JSON objects rather than HTML pages.

**Without this header:**
- A `404 Not Found` may return a Symfony HTML error page or Twig template.
- A `400 Bad Request` or `422` may return an HTML response that cannot be parsed by your client.
- A `500 Internal Server Error` returns an HTML debug page (in `APP_ENV=dev` mode).

**Always include this header on every request.**

### `Content-Type: application/json`

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Header name** | `Content-Type` |
| **Header value** | `application/json` |
| **Required for** | `POST` and `PATCH` requests that send a JSON body |

Tells the server the format of the data in the request body. Without this header on a POST or PATCH request, Symfony will not deserialize the JSON body — `$request->toArray()` or `$request->getContent()` will return an empty result, causing validation to fail with a `422` or `400` error.

> **Postman tip:** When you select **Body → raw → JSON** in Postman, the `Content-Type: application/json` header is added automatically. Verify it is present in the Headers tab before sending.

### `Authorization` (Future / Optional)

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Header name** | `Authorization` |
| **Header value** | `Bearer <token>` |
| **Required for** | Protected endpoints (if authentication is implemented) |

This API currently does not enforce authentication. If JWT or API token authentication is added in the future (e.g., via `lexik/jwt-authentication-bundle` or `symfony/security-bundle`), all mutating endpoints (`POST`, `PATCH`, `DELETE`) will require a valid Bearer token in this header. A missing or invalid token will return `401 Unauthorized`.

---

## Category Resource Schema

A **category** is the primary resource exposed by this API. Every endpoint that returns category data uses this schema.

| Field | Type | Nullable | Description |
|-------|------|----------|-------------|
| `id` | `integer` | No | Auto-incremented primary key. Assigned by the database on creation. Read-only — cannot be set or changed via the API. |
| `name` | `string` | No | Human-readable display name of the category. UTF-8 encoded. Required at creation. |
| `description` | `string` | Yes | Optional longer description of the category. Returns `null` if not provided at creation or if explicitly cleared. |
| `created_at` | `string` (ISO 8601) | No | UTC timestamp of when the record was created. Format: `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS+00:00`. Set automatically by Doctrine/Symfony. |
| `updated_at` | `string` (ISO 8601) | No | UTC timestamp of the last update. Equals `created_at` if the record has never been modified. Updated automatically on every save. |

**Example category object:**
```json
{
  "id": 1,
  "name": "Electronics",
  "description": "Gadgets, devices, and accessories",
  "created_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00+00:00",
  "updated_at": "2024-03-22T14:45:00+00:00"
}
```

> **Note on timestamps:** Symfony with Doctrine typically formats timestamps as `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS+00:00` (RFC 3339). This differs slightly from Laravel's `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS.000000Z` format. Both represent UTC time — adjust your client's date parser accordingly.

---

## Endpoint Reference

---

### 1. Get Categories

**Retrieve a list of all categories stored in the database.**

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Method** | `GET` |
| **Path** | `/api/categories` |
| **Full URL** | `http://localhost:8000/api/categories` |
| **Auth required** | No |
| **Request body** | None |

#### Purpose

This endpoint queries the `category` (or `categories`) database table via Doctrine ORM and returns every record as a JSON array. It is the primary read endpoint for listing all available categories. Use it to:

- Populate dropdown menus or select inputs in a frontend UI.
- Render a category listing page in an admin panel.
- Verify the current state of the database during development and testing.
- Retrieve all category IDs before making targeted `GET`, `PATCH`, or `DELETE` requests.

The response is always a JSON array — even if there are zero records, the server returns an empty array `[]` with a `200 OK` status. An empty collection is not an error condition.

#### Required Headers

| Header | Value | Required |
|--------|-------|----------|
| `Accept` | `application/json` | Yes |

#### URL Path Parameters

None. This endpoint operates on the entire collection, not a single resource.

#### Query Parameters

None defined by default. The base Symfony implementation does not include pagination, filtering, sorting, or search parameters unless explicitly added to the controller action.

#### Request Body

None. `GET` requests must not include a request body.

#### Success Response — `200 OK`

Returned when the server successfully queries the database, regardless of how many records exist.

**Response body:** A JSON array of category objects. Returns `[]` if no categories exist.

```json
[
  {
    "id": 1,
    "name": "Electronics",
    "description": "Gadgets, devices, and accessories",
    "created_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00+00:00",
    "updated_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00+00:00"
  },
  {
    "id": 2,
    "name": "Books",
    "description": null,
    "created_at": "2024-01-16T11:00:00+00:00",
    "updated_at": "2024-01-16T11:00:00+00:00"
  },
  {
    "id": 3,
    "name": "Clothing",
    "description": "Apparel and fashion items",
    "created_at": "2024-02-01T08:00:00+00:00",
    "updated_at": "2024-02-01T08:00:00+00:00"
  }
]
```

**Empty database response (still `200 OK`):**
```json
[]
```

#### Error Responses

| Code | Condition | Response Body |
|------|-----------|---------------|
| `500 Internal Server Error` | Database is unreachable, Doctrine entity mapping error, or unhandled PHP exception | `{"message": "Internal Server Error"}` |

#### Notes

- The order of records in the array is determined by the Doctrine repository's default ordering — typically ascending `id` (insertion order) unless a custom `ORDER BY` clause is defined in the repository.
- There is no pagination by default. If the `categories` table grows large, consider adding `?page=` and `?limit=` query parameters and using Doctrine's `Paginator` or the `KnpPaginatorBundle`.
- The `description` field may be `null` for any record — always handle this case in your client code to avoid null reference errors.
- This endpoint is **safe** (no side effects) and **idempotent** (calling it multiple times produces the same result, assuming no concurrent writes).

---

### 2. Post Category

**Create a new category resource in the database.**

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Method** | `POST` |
| **Path** | `/api/categories` |
| **Full URL** | `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories` |
| **Auth required** | No |
| **Request body** | JSON object |

#### Purpose

This endpoint accepts a JSON body containing the data for a new category, validates the input, persists a new `Category` entity to the database via Doctrine's `EntityManager`, and returns the newly created category object — including the auto-assigned `id` and timestamps generated at the time of insertion.

Use this endpoint whenever:
- A user creates a new category through an admin panel form or modal.
- A script or CLI tool needs to seed the database with initial category data.
- An integration test needs to create a category before testing other endpoints.

#### Required Headers

| Header | Value | Required |
|--------|-------|----------|
| `Accept` | `application/json` | Yes |
| `Content-Type` | `application/json` | Yes |

#### URL Path Parameters

None.

#### Request Body Fields

The request body must be a valid JSON object. The following fields are accepted:

| Field | Type | Required | Validation Rules | Description |
|-------|------|----------|-----------------|-------------|
| `name` | `string` | **Yes** | Required. Must be a non-empty string. Must not be blank. | The display name of the new category. |
| `description` | `string` | No | Optional. Must be a string if provided. May be `null` or omitted entirely. | A longer description of the category. |

**Minimal valid request body (name only):**
```json
{
  "name": "Sports"
}
```

**Full request body (name and description):**
```json
{
  "name": "Sports",
  "description": "Outdoor and fitness equipment"
}
```

**With explicit null description:**
```json
{
  "name": "Sports",
  "description": null
}
```

#### Success Response — `201 Created`

Returned when the category passes validation and is successfully inserted into the database.

**Response body:** The complete newly created category object, including the auto-assigned `id`, `created_at`, and `updated_at` timestamps.

```json
{
  "id": 5,
  "name": "Sports",
  "description": "Outdoor and fitness equipment",
  "created_at": "2024-06-01T12:00:00+00:00",
  "updated_at": "2024-06-01T12:00:00+00:00"
}
```

> **Important:** Save the returned `id` value. You will need it to reference this specific category in future `GET /api/categories/{id}`, `PATCH /api/categories/{id}`, and `DELETE /api/categories/{id}` requests.

#### Error Responses

| Code | Condition | Response Body |
|------|-----------|---------------|
| `400 Bad Request` | Request body is not valid JSON, or the JSON cannot be decoded | `{"message": "Bad Request"}` |
| `422 Unprocessable Entity` | Request body is valid JSON but fails validation (e.g., `name` is missing or blank) | See below |
| `500 Internal Server Error` | Database unreachable, Doctrine flush error, or unhandled exception | `{"message": "Internal Server Error"}` |

**`422` response body — missing `name` field:**
```json
{
  "message": "Validation failed.",
  "errors": {
    "name": [
      "This value should not be blank."
    ]
  }
}
```

**`422` response body — `name` is an empty string:**
```json
{
  "message": "Validation failed.",
  "errors": {
    "name": [
      "This value should not be blank."
    ]
  }
}
```

**`400` response body — malformed JSON:**
```json
{
  "message": "Bad Request"
}
```

The `errors` object maps each failing field name to an array of human-readable Symfony Validator constraint messages. Iterate over this object in your client to display inline validation feedback next to the relevant form fields.

#### Notes

- `created_at` and `updated_at` are set automatically by Doctrine lifecycle callbacks or Symfony's `TimestampableInterface`. Do not include them in the request body — they will be ignored.
- `id` is assigned by the database auto-increment sequence. Do not include it in the request body.
- If you omit `description`, the field is stored as `null` in the database and returned as `null` in all future responses for this record.
- `POST` is **not idempotent** — calling this endpoint twice with the same body will create two separate category records with different `id` values.

---

### 3. Single Category

**Retrieve a single category by its unique numeric ID.**

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Method** | `GET` |
| **Path** | `/api/categories/{id}` |
| **Full URL** | `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories/1` |
| **Auth required** | No |
| **Request body** | None |

#### Purpose

This endpoint looks up a single `Category` entity in the database by its primary key (`id`) using Doctrine's `EntityRepository::find()` (or Symfony's `#[MapEntity]` / `ParamConverter`) and returns it as a JSON object.

Use it when you need the full details of one specific category — for example:
- To populate a category detail or preview page.
- To pre-fill an edit form before a `PATCH` request.
- To verify that a category was created correctly after a `POST` request.
- To confirm a category still exists before attempting to update or delete it.

The `{id}` in the URL path is a **route parameter** — replace it with the actual integer primary key of the category you want to retrieve (e.g., `/api/categories/3` fetches the category with `id = 3`).

#### Required Headers

| Header | Value | Required |
|--------|-------|----------|
| `Accept` | `application/json` | Yes |

#### URL Path Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Required | Validation | Description |
|-----------|------|----------|------------|-------------|
| `id` | `integer` | **Yes** | Must be a positive integer. Must match an existing record in the database. | The unique numeric primary key of the category to retrieve. |

**Example — retrieve category with ID 7:**
```
GET http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories/7
```

#### Query Parameters

None.

#### Request Body

None. `GET` requests must not include a request body.

#### Success Response — `200 OK`

Returned when a category with the given `id` exists in the database.

**Response body:** A single category JSON object.

```json
{
  "id": 1,
  "name": "Electronics",
  "description": "Gadgets, devices, and accessories",
  "created_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00+00:00",
  "updated_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00+00:00"
}
```

#### Error Responses

| Code | Condition | Response Body |
|------|-----------|---------------|
| `404 Not Found` | No category with the given `id` exists in the database | `{"message": "Not Found"}` |
| `500 Internal Server Error` | Database unreachable or unhandled exception | `{"message": "Internal Server Error"}` |

**`404` response body:**
```json
{
  "message": "Not Found"
}
```

This response is returned when Symfony's controller cannot locate a `Category` entity with the specified ID. It does **not** mean the URL structure is wrong — it means the record does not exist in the database. This happens when:
- The category was never created.
- The category was previously deleted via `DELETE /api/categories/{id}`.
- You used the wrong ID value.

#### Notes

- The `{id}` must be a positive integer. Passing a non-integer value (e.g., `/api/categories/abc`) will result in a `404` because Symfony's router will not match the route pattern, or a `400` if the route matches but the type conversion fails.
- Use the `id` values returned by `GET /api/categories` or `POST /api/categories` to construct valid URLs for this endpoint.
- This endpoint is **safe** (no side effects) and **idempotent** — calling it multiple times with the same ID always returns the same result, assuming the record has not been modified or deleted.

---

### 4. Update Category

**Partially update an existing category by its unique numeric ID.**

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Method** | `PATCH` |
| **Path** | `/api/categories/{id}` |
| **Full URL** | `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories/1` |
| **Auth required** | No |
| **Request body** | JSON object (partial update) |

#### Purpose

This endpoint updates one or more fields of an existing `Category` entity. `PATCH` is a **partial update** method — you only need to include the fields you want to change in the request body. Fields you omit are left unchanged in the database.

Use this endpoint when:
- A user renames a category in an admin panel.
- A user updates a category's description without changing its name.
- A script needs to bulk-update category metadata.

**`PATCH` vs `PUT`:**
- `PUT` requires you to send the **complete** resource representation (all fields), effectively replacing the entire record. Any field omitted from a `PUT` body is typically set to `null` or its default value.
- `PATCH` only requires the fields you want to modify. Omitted fields retain their current database values.
- This API uses `PATCH` for partial updates, which is the recommended approach for most real-world update operations where you want to change only specific fields.

#### Required Headers

| Header | Value | Required |
|--------|-------|----------|
| `Accept` | `application/json` | Yes |
| `Content-Type` | `application/json` | Yes |

#### URL Path Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Required | Validation | Description |
|-----------|------|----------|------------|-------------|
| `id` | `integer` | **Yes** | Must be a positive integer. Must match an existing record. | The unique numeric primary key of the category to update. |

**Example — update category with ID 3:**
```
PATCH http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories/3
```

#### Request Body Fields

Send only the fields you want to update. All body fields are optional for `PATCH` requests.

| Field | Type | Required | Validation Rules | Description |
|-------|------|----------|-----------------|-------------|
| `name` | `string` | No | Must be a non-empty string if provided. Must not be blank. | The new display name for the category. |
| `description` | `string` or `null` | No | Must be a string or `null` if provided. | The new description. Pass `null` to explicitly clear the description. |

**Update only the name:**
```json
{
  "name": "Consumer Electronics"
}
```

**Update only the description:**
```json
{
  "description": "All electronic gadgets and devices"
}
```

**Update both fields simultaneously:**
```json
{
  "name": "Consumer Electronics",
  "description": "All electronic gadgets and devices"
}
```

**Clear the description (set to null):**
```json
{
  "description": null
}
```

#### Success Response — `200 OK`

Returned when the category is found, the request body passes validation, and the entity is successfully updated and flushed to the database.

**Response body:** The complete updated category object, reflecting all applied changes.

```json
{
  "id": 1,
  "name": "Consumer Electronics",
  "description": "All electronic gadgets and devices",
  "created_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00+00:00",
  "updated_at": "2024-06-18T10:00:00+00:00"
}
```

Note that `updated_at` is automatically set to the current UTC timestamp by Doctrine's lifecycle callbacks when the entity is saved. `created_at` remains unchanged.

#### Error Responses

| Code | Condition | Response Body |
|------|-----------|---------------|
| `400 Bad Request` | Request body is not valid JSON or cannot be decoded | `{"message": "Bad Request"}` |
| `404 Not Found` | No category with the given `id` exists in the database | `{"message": "Not Found"}` |
| `422 Unprocessable Entity` | A provided field fails validation (e.g., `name` is an empty string) | See below |
| `500 Internal Server Error` | Database unreachable, Doctrine flush error, or unhandled exception | `{"message": "Internal Server Error"}` |

**`404` response body:**
```json
{
  "message": "Not Found"
}
```

**`422` response body — `name` sent as empty string:**
```json
{
  "message": "Validation failed.",
  "errors": {
    "name": [
      "This value should not be blank."
    ]
  }
}
```

**`400` response body — malformed JSON body:**
```json
{
  "message": "Bad Request"
}
```

#### Notes

- Sending an empty JSON body `{}` is valid for `PATCH` — it results in no changes to the database and returns the current state of the category with `200 OK`.
- `id`, `created_at`, and `updated_at` are read-only. Even if you include them in the request body, they will be ignored by the controller.
- It is good practice to retrieve the current state of the category first (`GET /api/categories/{id}`) before sending a `PATCH` request, to avoid accidentally overwriting data you did not intend to change.
- `PATCH` is **idempotent** — sending the same `PATCH` request multiple times produces the same final state (though `updated_at` will be refreshed on each call).

---

### 5. Delete Category

**Permanently delete a category by its unique numeric ID.**

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Method** | `DELETE` |
| **Path** | `/api/categories/{id}` |
| **Full URL** | `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories/1` |
| **Auth required** | No |
| **Request body** | None |

#### Purpose

This endpoint permanently removes a `Category` entity from the database using Doctrine's `EntityManager::remove()` and `flush()`. The deletion is **irreversible by default** — once a category is deleted, it cannot be recovered unless you have a database backup or have implemented Doctrine's soft-delete extension (e.g., `gedmo/doctrine-extensions` with `SoftDeleteable`).

Use this endpoint when:
- A user removes a category from the admin panel.
- You need to clean up test data after running integration tests.
- A category is being merged into another and the old one must be removed.

> **Warning on referential integrity:** If other database tables reference the deleted category's `id` as a foreign key (e.g., a `product` table with a `category_id` column), deleting the category may cause a database constraint violation (`500` error) or leave orphaned records, depending on your foreign key `ON DELETE` configuration (`CASCADE`, `SET NULL`, `RESTRICT`). Always verify referential integrity before deleting categories in a production environment.

#### Required Headers

| Header | Value | Required |
|--------|-------|----------|
| `Accept` | `application/json` | Yes |

#### URL Path Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Required | Validation | Description |
|-----------|------|----------|------------|-------------|
| `id` | `integer` | **Yes** | Must be a positive integer. Must match an existing record. | The unique numeric primary key of the category to delete. |

**Example — delete category with ID 5:**
```
DELETE http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories/5
```

#### Query Parameters

None.

#### Request Body

None. `DELETE` requests do not require a request body.

#### Success Response — `200 OK` or `204 No Content`

The response code depends on the controller implementation:

**Option A — `204 No Content` (REST-standard, recommended):**

The server returns HTTP `204` with an empty body. This is the standard REST response for a successful deletion — the resource no longer exists, so there is nothing to return. In Postman, the response body panel will be empty.

**Option B — `200 OK` with a confirmation message:**

```json
{
  "message": "Category deleted successfully."
}
```

> Check your Symfony controller's `delete` action to confirm which response your implementation returns. Look for `return new Response(null, Response::HTTP_NO_CONTENT)` (204) or `return $this->json(['message' => '...'])` (200).

#### Error Responses

| Code | Condition | Response Body |
|------|-----------|---------------|
| `404 Not Found` | No category with the given `id` exists in the database | `{"message": "Not Found"}` |
| `500 Internal Server Error` | Database constraint violation (foreign key), unreachable database, or unhandled exception | `{"message": "Internal Server Error"}` |

**`404` response body:**
```json
{
  "message": "Not Found"
}
```

Returned when you attempt to delete a category that does not exist — for example, if it was already deleted, or if you used the wrong ID.

**`500` response body — foreign key constraint violation (in `APP_ENV=dev`):**
```json
{
  "message": "An exception occurred while executing a query: SQLSTATE[23000]: Integrity constraint violation: 1451 Cannot delete or update a parent row: a foreign key constraint fails",
  "class": "Doctrine\\DBAL\\Exception\\ForeignKeyConstraintViolationException"
}
```

In `APP_ENV=prod`, this returns a generic `{"message": "Internal Server Error"}` without the stack trace.

#### Notes

- Deletion is **permanent and irreversible** by default. To implement recoverable deletion, use the `gedmo/doctrine-extensions` `SoftDeleteable` behavior, which adds a `deleted_at` timestamp column and filters deleted records from all queries automatically.
- After a successful deletion, any subsequent `GET /api/categories/{id}` request for the same ID will return `404 Not Found`.
- The deleted category's `id` will **not** be reused by the database auto-increment sequence — the next created category will receive a higher ID.
- `DELETE` is **idempotent in intent** but not in practice with this API: the first call deletes the record and returns `204`/`200`; subsequent calls for the same ID return `404` because the record no longer exists.

---

## HTTP Status Code Reference

| Code | Name | Used By | Meaning in This API |
|------|------|---------|---------------------|
| `200` | OK | GET, PATCH, DELETE | Request succeeded. Response body contains the requested data, the updated object, or a confirmation message. |
| `201` | Created | POST | New category created successfully. Response body contains the new object with its auto-assigned `id`. |
| `204` | No Content | DELETE | Category deleted successfully. No response body is returned. |
| `400` | Bad Request | POST, PATCH | The request body is malformed — not valid JSON, or the JSON cannot be decoded by the server. |
| `401` | Unauthorized | Any (if auth added) | Authentication credentials are missing or invalid. Returned when a Symfony security firewall rejects the request. |
| `403` | Forbidden | Any (if auth added) | The request is authenticated but the user does not have permission to perform this action on this resource. |
| `404` | Not Found | GET, PATCH, DELETE | The URL does not match any registered Symfony route, or the requested resource ID does not exist in the database. |
| `422` | Unprocessable Entity | POST, PATCH | The request body is syntactically valid JSON but fails Symfony's Validator constraints. The `errors` object contains field-level messages. |
| `500` | Internal Server Error | Any | An unhandled server-side exception occurred. Check `var/log/dev.log` (or `prod.log`) for the full stack trace. |

---

## Error Response Structure

All error responses from this API follow a consistent JSON structure when the `Accept: application/json` header is present.

### Validation Error (`422`)

```json
{
  "message": "Validation failed.",
  "errors": {
    "name": [
      "This value should not be blank."
    ],
    "description": [
      "This value must be of type string."
    ]
  }
}
```

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `message` | `string` | A human-readable summary of the validation failure. |
| `errors` | `object` | A map of field names to arrays of Symfony Validator constraint violation messages. Each key is a request field that failed one or more constraints. |

### Not Found Error (`404`)

```json
{
  "message": "Not Found"
}
```

### Bad Request Error (`400`)

```json
{
  "message": "Bad Request"
}
```

### Server Error (`500`)

```json
{
  "message": "Internal Server Error"
}
```

In `APP_ENV=dev` mode, `500` errors also include `class`, `message` (the full exception message), and `trace` fields for debugging. In `APP_ENV=prod`, only the generic message is returned to avoid leaking implementation details.

---

## Usage Guidelines

### 1. Start the Symfony Development Server

```bash
# Using Symfony CLI (recommended)
symfony server:start

# Or using PHP's built-in server
php -S 127.0.0.1:8000 -t public/
```

The server starts at `http://127.0.0.1:8000` by default.

### 2. Always Include the Accept Header

Every request must include `Accept: application/json` to ensure all responses — including errors — are returned as JSON rather than HTML.

### 3. Use a Postman Environment for the Base URL

1. Create a Postman environment called `Symfony Local`.
2. Add a variable: `base_url` = `http://127.0.0.1:8000`.
3. Select the environment from the top-right dropdown in Postman.
4. Use `{{base_url}}/api/categories` in all request URLs.

### 4. Understand the Status Code Before Reading the Body

| Code | Action |
|------|--------|
| `200` | Read the array (GET all) or single/updated object (GET one, PATCH) |
| `201` | Read the created object and save the `id` for future requests |
| `204` | No body — deletion was successful |
| `400` | Fix the request body — it is not valid JSON |
| `404` | Fix the URL or verify the resource ID exists via `GET /api/categories` |
| `422` | Read the `errors` object and fix the request body fields |
| `500` | Check `var/log/dev.log` for the full exception and stack trace |

### 5. Database Setup with Doctrine

```bash
# Create the database
php bin/console doctrine:database:create

# Run migrations
php bin/console doctrine:migrations:migrate

# Optional: load fixtures (sample data)
php bin/console doctrine:fixtures:load
```

### 6. Environment Configuration (`.env`)

```env
APP_ENV=dev
APP_DEBUG=true
APP_SECRET=your-secret-key-here
DATABASE_URL="mysql://root:@127.0.0.1:3306/symfony_db?serverVersion=8.0"
```

After changing `.env`:
```bash
php bin/console cache:clear
```

### 7. Debugging Tips

- **`500` errors:** Check `var/log/dev.log` for the full exception message and stack trace.
- **Route not found (`404`):** Run `php bin/console debug:router` to list all registered routes and verify your URL matches.
- **Stale routes or config:** Run `php bin/console cache:clear` after adding new routes or changing configuration.
- **Validation not triggering:** Ensure `Content-Type: application/json` is set on POST/PATCH requests and the body is valid JSON.
- **Empty response body on DELETE:** Your controller likely returns `204 No Content` — this is correct REST behaviour.
- **Doctrine entity not found:** Ensure migrations have been run and the database contains the expected records.

---

## Getting Started Checklist

- [ ] Symfony development server is running: `symfony server:start` or `php -S 127.0.0.1:8000 -t public/`
- [ ] Database is running and `DATABASE_URL` in `.env` is correctly configured
- [ ] Doctrine migrations have been applied: `php bin/console doctrine:migrations:migrate`
- [ ] Postman environment `Symfony Local` is created with `base_url = http://127.0.0.1:8000`
- [ ] All requests include the `Accept: application/json` header
- [ ] POST and PATCH requests include `Content-Type: application/json` and a valid JSON body
- [ ] You have sent `GET /api/categories` and received a `200 OK` response with a JSON array

request.description:
# Get Categories

Retrieves a complete list of all category resources stored in the database. This is a **read-only** endpoint — it does not create, modify, or delete any data. The response is a JSON array where each element represents one category object.

---

## Endpoint

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Method** | `GET` |
| **URL** | `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories` |
| **Authentication** | None required |
| **Request Body** | None |
| **Idempotent** | Yes — calling this endpoint multiple times always returns the same result without side effects |

---

## Required Request Headers

The following headers must be included with every request to this endpoint.

| Header | Value | Required | Purpose |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `Accept` | `application/json` | **Yes** | Instructs the server to return the response as JSON. Without this header, Symfony may return an HTML page instead of structured data — especially for error responses like 404 and 500. |

**How to add this header in Postman:**

1. Open this request in Postman.
2. Click the **Headers** tab (next to Params, Authorization, Body).
3. In an empty row, enter:
   - **Key:** `Accept`
   - **Value:** `application/json`
4. Make sure the checkbox on the left side of the row is **checked** (enabled).
5. Click **Send**.

> `Content-Type` is **not** required for this request because `GET` requests do not have a request body. Only include `Content-Type: application/json` on requests that send a body (POST, PUT, PATCH).

---

## Query Parameters

This endpoint does not accept any query parameters. The full list of categories is always returned. Filtering, sorting, and pagination are not currently implemented.

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| *(none)* | — | — | No query parameters are supported |

---

## JSON Response Structure

A successful `200 OK` response returns a **JSON array** at the top level. Each element of the array is a **category object**.

### Category Object Fields

| Field | Type | Nullable | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `id` | `integer` | No | The unique numeric identifier for this category. Auto-assigned by the database. Immutable after creation. |
| `name` | `string` | No | The human-readable display name of the category. UTF-8 encoded. |

### `id` — Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** Positive integer (e.g., `1`, `7`, `42`)
- **Assigned by:** The database engine automatically, using an auto-increment primary key
- **Immutable:** Yes — this value is set once at creation and never changes, even if the category's `name` is updated
- **Unique:** Guaranteed to be unique across all category records
- **Never zero or negative** in a real database
- **Primary use:** Reference this value when building URLs for single-resource endpoints, e.g.:
  ```
  GET /api/categories/1
  PUT /api/categories/1
  DELETE /api/categories/1
  ```
- **In your front-end:** Store this value as the key in dropdowns, lists, and state management so you can identify which category the user selected

### `name` — Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** UTF-8 string
- **Set by:** The application when a category is created or updated
- **Mutable:** Yes — can be changed via `PUT` or `PATCH` endpoints
- **Uniqueness:** Not guaranteed at the API level (depends on database constraints)
- **May contain:** Spaces, punctuation, accented characters, Unicode (e.g., `"Électronique"`, `"Bücher"`, `"Категорія"`)
- **Primary use:** Display to end users in navigation menus, filter dropdowns, product listings, etc.

### Full Response Example (with data)

```json
[
  {
    "id": 1,
    "name": "Electronics"
  },
  {
    "id": 2,
    "name": "Books"
  },
  {
    "id": 3,
    "name": "Clothing"
  },
  {
    "id": 4,
    "name": "Home & Garden"
  }
]
```

### Empty Response Example

```json
[]
```

An empty array is a **valid, successful response**. It means the `categories` table in the database exists but contains no rows. The HTTP status code will still be `200 OK`. This is not an error — do not treat it as one in your application code.

---

## HTTP Status Codes

### 200 OK

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `200` |
| **Meaning** | Request succeeded. The server queried the database and returned the result. |
| **When you see it** | The Symfony server is running, the database is reachable, and the route is correctly registered |

**Example response:**
```json
[
  { "id": 1, "name": "Electronics" },
  { "id": 2, "name": "Books" }
]
```

**What to do:** Read the array and use the data. If the array is empty (`[]`), it means no categories have been created yet — this is still a success.

---

### 404 Not Found

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `404` |
| **Meaning** | The URL you requested does not match any route registered in Symfony |
| **When you see it** | URL typo, wrong path, or Symfony route cache is stale |

**Common causes for this endpoint:**
- Typing `/api/category` instead of `/api/categories` (missing the `s`)
- Typing `/api/Categories` (wrong case — URLs are case-sensitive)
- The Symfony server is running but the route was not registered (missing annotation/attribute or YAML config)
- The route cache is outdated after a code change

**Example response body (when `Accept: application/json` is set):**
```json
{
  "type": "https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2616#section-10",
  "title": "An error occurred",
  "detail": "No route found for \"GET /api/category\"",
  "status": 404
}
```

**How to fix:**
1. Verify the exact URL: `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories`
2. Run this command to list all registered routes and confirm the path:
   ```bash
   php bin/console debug:router
   ```
3. If you recently changed route configuration, clear the cache:
   ```bash
   php bin/console cache:clear
   ```

---

### 500 Internal Server Error

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `500` |
| **Meaning** | The server encountered an unexpected error while processing the request |
| **When you see it** | Database connection failure, PHP exception in the controller, misconfigured service |

**Common causes for this endpoint:**
- The database server (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite) is not running
- The `DATABASE_URL` in `.env` or `.env.local` is incorrect
- The `categories` table does not exist (migrations not run)
- A PHP exception is thrown inside the `CategoryController`
- Symfony's service container is misconfigured

**Example response body (when `Accept: application/json` is set):**
```json
{
  "type": "https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2616#section-10",
  "title": "An error occurred",
  "detail": "An exception occurred in the driver: SQLSTATE[HY000] [2002] Connection refused",
  "status": 500
}
```

**How to fix:**
1. Confirm your database server is running.
2. Check `DATABASE_URL` in `.env`:
   ```
   DATABASE_URL="mysql://root:password@127.0.0.1:3306/my_database"
   ```
3. Run pending migrations:
   ```bash
   php bin/console doctrine:migrations:migrate
   ```
4. Open the Symfony profiler for a full stack trace:
   ```
   http://127.0.0.1:8000/_profiler
   ```
5. Tail the Symfony log file in real time:
   ```bash
   tail -f var/log/dev.log
   ```

---

## Usage Guidelines

### Sending the Request from JavaScript (fetch)

```javascript
const response = await fetch('http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories', {
  method: 'GET',
  headers: {
    'Accept': 'application/json'
  }
});

if (!response.ok) {
  throw new Error(`HTTP error: ${response.status}`);
}

const categories = await response.json();
console.log(categories);
// Output: [{ id: 1, name: 'Electronics' }, { id: 2, name: 'Books' }, ...]
```

### Using the `id` Field in Subsequent Requests

The `id` returned by this endpoint is the key you use to interact with individual categories in other endpoints:

```
GET    /api/categories/1      → fetch a single category
PUT    /api/categories/1      → update a category's name
DELETE /api/categories/1      → delete a category
```

Always use the `id` from the API response — never hardcode IDs in your application, as they may differ between development, staging, and production databases.

### Handling an Empty Array

If the response is `[]`, do not show an error to the user. Instead, show an empty state message:

```javascript
if (categories.length === 0) {
  console.log('No categories found. Create one to get started.');
} else {
  // render the list
}
```

### Postman Test Scripts

Add these to the **Scripts → Post-response** tab of this request to automatically validate the response every time you send it:

```javascript
pm.test('Status code is 200', function () {
  pm.response.to.have.status(200);
});

pm.test('Response is a JSON array', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  pm.expect(body).to.be.an('array');
});

pm.test('Each category has id (number) and name (string)', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  body.forEach(function (category) {
    pm.expect(category).to.have.property('id').that.is.a('number');
    pm.expect(category).to.have.property('name').that.is.a('string');
  });
});
```

---

## Prerequisites Checklist

Before sending this request, confirm:

- [ ] Symfony server is running: `symfony server:start` or `php -S 127.0.0.1:8000 -t public/`
- [ ] Database server is running and accessible
- [ ] `DATABASE_URL` is correctly set in `.env` or `.env.local`
- [ ] Migrations have been applied: `php bin/console doctrine:migrations:migrate`
- [ ] The `Accept: application/json` header is present and enabled in the Headers tab
- [ ] The correct Postman environment is selected (if using `{{base_url}}`)
